items.q: What is a self-fulfilling prophecy in education?
items.correct: When teachers' expectations (often based on labels) influence pupil behaviour and achievement — pupils labelled as 'bright' tend to do better, those labelled 'thick' worse, regardless of underlying ability (Rosenthal and Jacobson).
items.misconception: A self-fulfilling prophecy is when a student predicts their own grade correctly.
items.random_wrong: A self-fulfilling prophecy is when teachers predict the future accurately.
items.why: The mechanism is the LABEL changing teacher behaviour, which changes pupil outcomes — not students predicting themselves. Students miss the causal chain (label → interaction → outcome) that makes the concept analytically powerful.

items.q: What did Murdock claim about the nuclear family?
items.correct: It is universal and performs four essential functions in all societies.
items.misconception: It is the only valid family form and others are dysfunctional.
items.random_wrong: It is a recent invention of capitalism.
items.why: Students confuse Murdock's empirical claim (universality) with a normative claim (it SHOULD be this way). His argument was descriptive functionalism, later critiqued by feminists and historians.

items.q: What do feminists mean by the 'dark side' of family life?
items.correct: The reality that families can be sites of inequality, exploitation and violence — particularly affecting women and children — challenging functionalist portrayals of the family as universally beneficial.
items.misconception: The 'dark side' refers to families who live in poverty.
items.random_wrong: The 'dark side' is a metaphor about families who don't communicate.
items.why: The feminist critique specifically targets gendered inequalities and domestic abuse hidden behind the functionalist 'warm bath' image. Students miss the structural critique and reduce it to general hardship.
Correct answer: Cooley. “Marx” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: A stage performance. “A factory” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many 'stages' does Goffman describe in dramaturgy (front and back)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
